metadata.purpose: Tylopilus and closely associated dark- or pink-pored bolete records.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Bitter Bolete is treated here as the Tylopilus felleus concept: a bitter, pink-pored bolete often confused with porcini-type mushrooms. North American material may not all be true European T. felleus, so use this page as a field-guide concept rather than a final microscope-level taxonomy claim.
records.culinary_uses: Niche edible with an intensely bitter flavor. Most foragers skip it because even a small amount can make a dish unpleasant, but it is retained in the edible mushroom list as an acquired-taste bittering mushroom rather than a poison/caution species.
records.edibility_detail: Use only if intentionally seeking strong bitterness; otherwise keep it separate from good edible boletes because a small piece can dominate a pan.
records.general_notes: Key field clues: pinkish pores as it matures, a strongly bitter nibble-and-spit taste, and dark netting on the stalk. Often confused with porcini/king bolete types.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through October in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
mushroom_profile.summary: Pink-pored Tylopilus with an intensely bitter taste. Not poisonous in the usual field-guide sense, but most foragers skip it because it can ruin a meal; kept here as a niche/acquired-taste edible.

records.poisoning_effects: Large amounts may cause stomach upset in some people; the practical problem is intense bitterness and confusion with better edible porcini-type boletes.
records.field_identification: Tan to brown bolete with a strong dark net pattern on the stem, whitish pores that mature pinkish, and an unmistakably bitter taste. Often under conifers or mixed woods.
records.look_alike_notes: Important king-bolete confusion risk: bitter bolete has pinkish mature pores and a bitter taste; do not mix it into a pan of good boletes unless you truly want bitterness.
records.edibility_notes: Inedible because of intense bitterness rather than dangerous poisoning. Keep it out of the Caution page unless a future record adds a real toxicity concern.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus felleus
use_links.notes: Pink pore surface, bitter taste, conifer association, and Michigan taxonomic note.

records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Bitter Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.
records.toxicity_notes: Not treated as a poison species in this guide; retained as a niche/acquired-taste edible with a strong bitter flavor.

records.quality_notes: Very bitter; even a small piece can ruin a pan of good boletes.
records.eating_use_notes: Keep separate from edible porcini-type boletes unless intentionally using bitterness.
records.risk_reason: Practical risk is spoilage of a meal and confusion with porcini-type boletes, not a deadly toxin posture in this guide.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Pinkish mature pores, strong dark netting on the stalk, and bitter taste separate it from king bolete types.
records.hard_rule: If a porcini-like bolete has pinkish pores or tastes bitter during a tiny spit-out taste test, do not put it in the food basket.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed brownish-pink spore print and corrects this hidden legacy duplicate to a non-food avoid role; no classifier behavior was changed.

records.culinary_uses: Bitter; not a food target. One bitter bolete can wreck a meal.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; northeastern/Midwestern distribution noted in keys.
research_notes: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.

records.edibility_detail: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
records.mushroom_card_note: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
records.field_identification: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.
records.risk_reason: The main issue is bitterness and bitter-bolete confusion; it is included so a bitter Tylopilus does not ruin a meal.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.

records.source_note: v4.3.211 adds an exact-species or defensible complex-level macroscopic spore-print description and normalized filter values; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Often treated as a good edible bolete with a mild taste, but use only with confident ID and watch for bitter Tylopilus look-alikes.
records.notes: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black.

mushroom_profile.summary: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black. Commons-backed images added.
research_notes: Dark blackish velvety cap, dark stem, white pore surface becoming pinkish, and bruising that turns red then brown/black are the key field marks. Often under oaks.
Look for the blackish velvety cap, dark smooth stem, white pore surface that matures pinkish, and red-to-black bruising. Often with oaks; older guides list it as Tylopilus alboater.

records.edibility_detail: Use only after confirming dark velvety cap/stem, pore-color changes, and red-to-black bruising pattern.
records.mushroom_card_note: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black. Commons-backed images added.
records.field_identification: Look for the blackish velvety cap, dark smooth stem, white pore surface that matures pinkish, and red-to-black bruising. Often with oaks; older guides list it as Tylopilus alboater.

records.quality_notes: Dark Tylopilus-like bolete with fair-to-good food value only after advanced confirmation.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed specimens; do not use as a casual brown/dark bolete.
records.risk_reason: Risk is advanced ID: dark boletes and Tylopilus-like species can be confusing and some are poor food or not food targets.
records.hard_rule: Use only after confirming dark velvety cap/stem, pore-color changes, and red-to-black bruising pattern.

edible_use.notes: Cook confirmed specimens; do not use as a casual brown/dark bolete.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food mushroom in this guide; keep as a cautious/ID record unless a local expert confirms the collection.
records.notes: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.

mushroom_profile.summary: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Check for a dusky cap, brownish pore surface, dark spore-print group, and flesh/stem staining tendencies. It is easy to confuse with other dark Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-like boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.source_note: v4.3.211 restores review-needed status: the exact species is recognized in a North American key, but a macroscopic print and Great Lakes occurrence remain unverified.

records.culinary_uses: Sometimes considered edible when confidently identified, but this guide treats it as a cautious bolete because Tylopilus look-alikes can be bitter or confusing.

mushroom_profile.summary: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; useful as a comparison species around bitter boletes.
staining: Pores bruise brown; flesh may slowly stain pinkish
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.

records.edibility_detail: Do not present as beginner food; confirm maroon cap, pinkish-tan pores, brown-bruising pores, hardwood/oak association, and mild-not-bitter taste.
records.field_identification: Look for a maroon to reddish-brown velvety cap, whitish pores becoming pinkish-tan, brown-bruising pores, and oak/hardwood association. Taste should be mild, not strongly bitter.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Oak association, mild taste, pore color, and brown bruising.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, mild brown-and-white Tylopilus traits, Tylopilus ferrugineus confusion, oak association, and edible-use as Good.

records.edibility_notes: Mild-tasting Tylopilus; edible reports exist, but confirm it is not a bitter Tylopilus before using.

edible_use.notes: Cook only confirmed mild specimens; skip if bitter, old, buggy, or uncertain.

records.mushroom_card_note: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; not the bitter-bolete disaster, but still an ID-first bolete.

records.quality_notes: Niche mild Tylopilus with edible-use source support; keep mild-not-bitter and expert-ID warnings. Not a beginner target.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook only confirmed mild specimens; skip if bitter, old, buggy, or uncertain.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusing mild niche Tylopilus with bitter or poorly understood Tylopilus species.
records.hard_rule: Do not present as beginner food; confirm maroon cap, pinkish-tan pores, brown-bruising pores, hardwood/oak association, and mild-not-bitter taste.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.notes: Tylopilus ferrugineus: rusty-brown mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores; advanced ID, not a casual basket filler.

records.mushroom_card_note: Bitter Tylopilus with violet/lilac tones when young; useful to recognize because pretty does not mean edible.
records.field_identification: Young stem shows purple to lilac shades; cap can bruise rusty purple to dark purple, with Tylopilus pinkish pore maturity and bitter taste.
